---
title: "Migrate from RStudio to Positron"
description: "Set up Positron so it looks, feels, and responds like the RStudio you already know. A hands-on tutorial for RStudio users."
---

::: {.callout-important}
**Should you take this tutorial first?** This tutorial assumes Positron is installed and you have a project open. If that is not the case, start with [First data analysis with R in a Quarto document](tutorial-get-started-quarto.qmd). It takes about 15 minutes and leaves you with a project you can configure here.
:::

You just finished your first analysis in Positron. If some of it felt unfamiliar compared to RStudio, that is completely normal, and it is exactly what this tutorial fixes.

**You are fast in RStudio because of muscle memory, not because of RStudio itself.** In RStudio, you know where everything is. Over the next 20 minutes you will rebuild that familiarity by configuring Positron to be similar to RStudio. Along the way you will meet a few things Positron can do that RStudio cannot.

We will suggest specific settings that work well for most RStudio users, but you can change these settings however you like. Think of this as your chance to make Positron your personalized IDE.

::: {.callout-important}
**RStudio is not going away.** It is actively maintained with no deprecation timeline. Moving to Positron is not all-or-nothing; it is fine to use both IDEs side by side, and this tutorial will point out the few tasks where you might still prefer RStudio.
:::

# Start with a new habit: the Command Palette

In RStudio, when you want to do something, you reach for a button, a menu, or a keyboard shortcut you have memorized. Positron has hundreds of commands too, but instead of memorizing where each one lives, you learn **one place to find them all**: the Command Palette.

The Command Palette is a searchable list of every IDE command you can run in Positron. Open it with the keyboard shortcut {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-P win=Ctrl-Shift-P linux=Ctrl-Shift-P >}}.

Start typing a keyword and the fuzzy finder shows matching commands. Use the arrow keys to move through them, then press {{< kbd Enter >}} to run one.

This is the single most valuable habit to build for Positron. For the rest of this tutorial, almost every step starts the same way: **open the Command Palette and type what you want.** That one reflex replaces most of the buttons you used to hunt for in RStudio. By the end of this tutorial, you will be opening it without thinking.

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
Open the Command Palette, type _Toggle Primary Side Bar_, and press {{< kbd Enter >}}.

**You will know it worked when** the side bar on the left disappears. Run the same command again to bring it back. You just did something without touching a single button.
:::

This video shows how to open the Command Palette, type a keyword, and run a command.

{{< video videos/tutorial-migrate-command-palette.mp4 aria-label="Opening the Command Palette in Positron, typing a keyword, and executing a command" >}}


::: {.callout-important}
Positron also ships with an interactive **Migrating from RStudio to Positron** walkthrough. Open it anytime from the Command Palette by searching for _Welcome: Open Walkthrough_ and selecting it. Treat it as a quick-reference companion to this tutorial, and notice that you just used the Command Palette to find it.
:::

# Bring your keyboard shortcuts with you

Your fingers already know {{< kbd mac=Command-Enter win=Ctrl-Enter linux=Ctrl-Enter >}} to run a line and {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-M win=Ctrl-Shift-M linux=Ctrl-Shift-M >}} to insert a pipe. Losing that muscle memory is the productivity fear most RStudio users have, so let's settle it first.

Many RStudio shortcuts already work in Positron unchanged. You can turn on RStudio's full shortcut set with a single setting. **We recommend turning it on** if you are mainly familiar with RStudio as your IDE of choice. Enable [`workbench.keybindings.rstudioKeybindings`](positron://settings/workbench.keybindings.rstudioKeybindings), then restart Positron for it to take effect.

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
Open the Command Palette, search for _Preferences: Open Settings (UI)_, and search the settings for `rstudioKeybindings`. Turn it on, then restart Positron by running _Developer: Reload Window_ from the Command Palette.

**You will know it worked when** {{< kbd Ctrl-1 >}} moves your cursor to the editor and {{< kbd Ctrl-2 >}} jumps to the Console after the restart.
:::

Once enabled, these RStudio shortcuts are available in Positron:

| Shortcut | Description |
| -------- | ----------- |
| {{< kbd Ctrl-1 >}} | Focus Source |
| {{< kbd Ctrl-2 >}} | Focus Console |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-. win=Ctrl-. linux=Ctrl-. >}} | Go to Function/Symbol |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-C win=Ctrl-Shift-C linux=Ctrl-Shift-C >}} | Comment/Uncomment a line |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-N win=Ctrl-Shift-N linux=Ctrl-Shift-N >}} | Create a new R file |
| {{< kbd F2 >}} | Go to definition |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-I win=Ctrl-I linux=Ctrl-I >}} | Reindent selection |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-A win=Ctrl-Shift-A linux=Ctrl-Shift-A >}} | Reformat selection |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-S win=Ctrl-Shift-S linux=Ctrl-Shift-S >}} | Source the current R script |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Option-Shift-M win=Ctrl-Alt-Shift-M linux=Ctrl-Alt-Shift-M >}} | Rename symbol |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Option-I win=Ctrl-Alt-I linux=Ctrl-Alt-I >}} | Insert code cell (Quarto) |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Enter win=Ctrl-Enter linux=Ctrl-Enter >}} | Run current statement (Quarto) |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-Enter win=Ctrl-Shift-Enter linux=Ctrl-Shift-Enter >}} | Run current cell (Quarto) |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Option-M win=Ctrl-Alt-M linux=Ctrl-Alt-M >}} | Open version control pane |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Option-← win=Ctrl-Alt-← linux=Ctrl-Alt-← >}} | Go to previous tab |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Option-→ win=Ctrl-Alt-→ linux=Ctrl-Alt-→ >}} | Go to next tab |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-D win=Ctrl-D linux=Ctrl-D >}} | Delete the current line |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-H win=Ctrl-Shift-H linux=Ctrl-Shift-H >}} | Set working directory |
| {{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-R win=Ctrl-Shift-R linux=Ctrl-Shift-R >}} | Insert section |
| {{< kbd mac=Option-Shift-K win=Alt-Shift-K linux=Alt-Shift-K >}} | Open global keybindings list |

This video shows how to enable RStudio keybindings and confirm a familiar shortcut works.

{{< video videos/tutorial-migrate-rstudio-keybindings.mp4 aria-label="Enabling RStudio keybindings in Positron settings and confirming Ctrl-1 and Ctrl-2 move focus between the editor and console" >}}


::: {.callout-important}
**Prefer to learn Positron's native shortcuts?** Leave this setting off. See [Positron keyboard shortcuts](keyboard-shortcuts.qmd) and [R-specific shortcuts](guide-r.qmd#keyboard-shortcuts) for the full reference, and remember you can always look up and change any command's shortcut in the Command Palette.

:::

![Keyboard shortcuts as they appear in the Command Palette](images/tutorial-keyboard-shortcuts-command-palette.png){fig-alt="A screenshot of the Command Palette with Configure Keybinding option."}

# Make Positron feel familiar

With the Command Palette and your shortcuts in place, let's make Positron *look* like home.

## Open your existing RStudio projects

In RStudio, you open a project by double-clicking its `.Rproj` file. In Positron, a **workspace** is simply a folder opened in its own window: the same idea, without the special file.

To open one of your existing projects, use *File: Open Folder* in the Command Palette (or **File > Open Folder**) and choose the project folder. You can also run `positron .` from a terminal inside the folder.

Your existing `.Rproj` file is safe to leave in place, Positron ignores it harmlessly. If you use Git, your `.git/` folder continues to mark the project root for tools like the [here](https://here.r-lib.org/) package, so paths keep working exactly as before.

![Various ways to open a folder in Positron, highlighted in orange.](images/open-folder.png){fig-alt="Positron Welcome screen with Open button and folder dropdown highlighted in orange, showing options like New Folder, Open Folder, and recent folders list."}

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
Open the Command Palette and search for _File: Open Recent_. Reopen a project you already work in.

**You will know it worked when** the project opens in its own window with your files in the Explorer, no `.Rproj` required. Rerun _File: Open Recent_ to return to your previous project.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
Positron stores project-specific settings in `.vscode/settings.json`, but only creates that file once you configure something project-specific. There is no Positron equivalent of the `.Rproj` file, and you do not need one.
:::

## Match your layout

RStudio arranges everything into four fixed panes. Positron uses the same building blocks, an Editor, a Console, and panes for Variables, Plots, and more, but gives you two new controls for arranging them:

- The **Activity Bar** (the icon strip on the far left) chooses what the Primary Side Bar shows.
- The **Primary Side Bar** displays context-specific content (files, search, Git, extensions) depending on the active Activity Bar icon.

Here is how the Activity Bar maps to tools you know from RStudio:

| Activity Bar icon | RStudio equivalent |
|---|---|
| Explorer {{< fa copy >}} | Files pane and document Outline |
| Search {{< fa magnifying-glass >}} | *Edit > Find in Files* ({{< kbd mac=Command-Shift-F win=Ctrl-Shift-F linux=Ctrl-Shift-F >}}) |
| Source Control {{< fa code-branch >}} | Git pane (see [using Git in Positron](git.qmd)) |
| Debugger | Debugging pane when in debugger mode |
| Test Explorer {{< fa flask >}} | *(New in Positron — no RStudio equivalent)* |
| Posit Assistant | Posit Assistant Chat window  |
| Publisher | Publishing Wizard |
| Packages | Packages pane |

![Interface of the Positron IDE](images/user-interface-for-rstudio-migration.jpeg){fig-alt="A screenshot with labelled boxes around the Activity bar, Primary side bar, Editor, Panel, and Secondary sidebar."}

Positron's layout is more flexible than RStudio's. **We recommend starting from a built-in preset** and adjusting from there.

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
Open the Command Palette and type `layout`. Try the **Stacked**, **Side-by-Side**, and **Notebook** presets.

**You will know it worked when** the panes rearrange instantly. Keep the one that feels most like your RStudio setup. You can also drag any pane to a new spot.

:::

The video below shows how to drag a pane to a new spot.

{{< video videos/tutorial-migrate-rstudio-drag-pane.mp4 aria-label="Drag any pane to a new spot." >}}

You can also adjust the layout with the icons at the top right of Positron.

![Layout buttons](images/tutorial-layout-buttons.png){fig-alt="A screenshot of Positron with the layout buttons at the top right of the IDE labelled."}

::: {.callout-important}
See [Customize the Layout](layout.qmd) for the full set of options.
:::

## Bring over your theme

A familiar color scheme goes a long way. If you like RStudio's default editor colors, install the [Tomorrow Night Bright (R Classic)](https://open-vsx.org/extension/gvelasq/tomorrow-night-bright-r-classic) theme (see [Add features with extensions](#add-features-with-extensions) below). Otherwise, Positron ships with dozens of built-in themes.

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
Open the Command Palette and search for _Preferences: Color Theme_. Use the arrow keys to move through the list.

**You will know it worked when** the whole editor recolors *live* as you move, a small taste of how much Positron previews before you commit. Press {{< kbd Enter >}} on the one you want.



:::

The video below shows how to preview color themes with _Preferences: Color Theme_.

{{< video videos/tutorial-migrate-color-themes.mp4 aria-label="Use _Preferences: Color Theme_ to preview color themes." >}}

# Make Positron more powerful than RStudio

Now that Positron feels familiar, let's set up a few things that go beyond what RStudio offers.

## Format your code automatically with Air

In RStudio you might reformat code by hand or run [styler](https://styler.r-lib.org/). Positron bundles [Air](https://posit-dev.github.io/air/), an R formatter, for `.R`, `.qmd`, and `.Rmd` files.

- To format a file, open the Command Palette and run _Format Document_.
- To format a selection, run _Format Selection_.

**We recommend enabling Format on Save** so your code is tidied automatically every time you save — no shortcut to remember.

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
Open the Command Palette, search for _Preferences: Open Settings (UI)_, and turn on [`editor.formatOnSave`](positron://settings/editor.formatOnSave). Then open an R file, add some messy spacing, and save with {{< kbd mac=Command-S win=Ctrl-S linux=Ctrl-S >}}.

**You will know it worked when** Air snaps the code into clean formatting the instant you save.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
See the [Air documentation](https://posit-dev.github.io/air/) for details on choosing a user vs. workspace setting.
:::

## Reuse code with snippets

Code snippets insert ready-made templates for common code patterns. For example, typing `for` and selecting the snippet inserts this skeleton:

```r
for (variable in vector) {
    # code to repeat
}
```

Press {{< kbd Tab >}} to jump between the placeholders for `variable`, `vector`, and `# code to repeat`.

![Completion list after typing "for", showing the snippet that can be inserted](images/snippets-for-example.png){width=700}

Positron provides snippets for R's reserved words (`if`, `while`, `for`, `function`) out of the box.

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
In an R file, type `for` and select the snippet from the completion list. Press {{< kbd Tab >}} to move through each placeholder.

**You will know it worked when** the cursor jumps cleanly from `variable` to `vector` to the body.
:::

To add your own snippets, open the Command Palette and run _Snippets: Configure Snippets_. Positron uses the [TextMate snippet syntax](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editing/userdefinedsnippets), which differs from RStudio's format. You can find RStudio's default snippets already translated to Positron's syntax in [`r.code-snippets`](https://github.com/posit-dev/ark/blob/19337a1b41e8c5a3a77ac61db93b7c6bf6cdc8a3/crates/ark/resources/snippets/r.code-snippets)(on Github).


## Add features with extensions

Extensions let you add features to Positron beyond its defaults. In this way, they are like RStudio addins, but the Extensions marketplace is far larger. Open the Extensions view from the Activity Bar, or run _Extensions: Focus on Extensions View_ from the Command Palette. Then search for extension by name or keyword.

Some popular picks for R users:

| Extension | Purpose |
|---|---|
| [Tomorrow Night Bright (R Classic)](https://open-vsx.org/extension/gvelasq/tomorrow-night-bright-r-classic) | RStudio dark theme ported to Positron |
| [Indent-Rainbow](https://open-vsx.org/extension/oderwat/indent-rainbow) | Colorized indentation |
| [Rainbow CSV](https://open-vsx.org/extension/mechatroner/rainbow-csv) | Colorized CSV file editing |
| [GitHub Actions](https://open-vsx.org/extension/GitHub/vscode-github-actions) | Interact with GitHub Actions from the IDE |
| [GitLab Workflow](https://open-vsx.org/extension/GitLab/gitlab-workflow) | GitLab support |
| [Project Manager](https://open-vsx.org/extension/alefragnani/project-manager) | Quickly switch between projects |

::: {.callout-tip title="Try it now"}
Open the Command Palette, run _Extensions: Focus on Extensions View_, and search for `Tomorrow Night Bright (R Classic)`. Click **Install**.

**You will know it worked when** you can select it from _Preferences: Color Theme_ and your editor takes on RStudio's classic dark mode colors. Scroll to _Light_ to return to Posit's default colors.


:::

The video below demonstrates how to find and install an extension from the marketplace.

{{< video videos/tutorial-migrate-extensions.mp4 aria-label="Opening the Positron extension gallery, searching for an extension, and installing it" >}}

You can also [create your own extensions](extension-development.qmd) with TypeScript/JavaScript, going well beyond what RStudio addins could do.

::: {.callout-important}
Many software companies provide free extensions that let you run their software inside of Positron, such as SAS, Snowflake, Databricks, and DuckDb. If you use specific software in your workflow, check for it in the Extension Marketplace.
:::

## Run your RStudio add-ins

Positron supports most [RStudio addins](https://rstudio.github.io/rstudio-extensions/rstudio_addins.html) through shims for the [rstudioapi](https://rstudio.github.io/rstudioapi/) package. To run one, open the Command Palette and run _R: Run RStudio Addin_. For example, with the [reprex](https://reprex.tidyverse.org/) package installed, you can launch its addin to build a reproducible example.

Not every rstudioapi function is implemented, so use `rstudioapi::hasFun()` to check whether a specific method is available before calling it. To detect programmatically whether your code is running in Positron, check `Sys.getenv("POSITRON")`.

# Know the gaps

Part of migrating confidently is knowing exactly where the edges are, so nothing surprises you later.

## What's not in Positron (and what to do instead)

A few RStudio features are not yet in Positron. If any of these are essential to your workflow, it is perfectly reasonable to keep using RStudio for that task:

- **Saving and reloading workspace state on R restart.** Positron starts each session clean; rely on your scripts to rebuild state.
- **Dedicated panes and buttons for code profiling and package development.** The underlying tooling still works from the Console and Command Palette.
- **A data import widget.** Follow progress on [GitHub](https://github.com/posit-dev/positron/issues/5515); in the meantime, use `readr` / `readxl` in code.

## What Positron does that RStudio cannot

And here is what you gain by making the move:

- Easy access to [multiple R versions](r-installations.qmd) and [multiple concurrent R sessions](managing-interpreters.qmd) in one window.
- If a session crashes, open a new one without restarting the IDE.
- Python as a first-class citizen, with data science support on par with R and the ability to run R and Python in the same session.
- An integrated [Data Explorer](data-explorer.qmd) for data frames and files (CSV, Parquet, etc.).
- A large third-party extension marketplace, with tools from providers like SAS, Snowflake, and Databricks.
- [Remote sessions](remote-ssh.qmd) in Posit Workbench, where the UI is local but compute runs on a remote server.

::: {.callout-important}
If you ever miss a button or pane from RStudio, check the Command Palette first. Almost every action in Positron lives there, and your familiar keyboard shortcuts usually work too.
:::

# Your forever Positron is ready

You have rebuilt your home: the Command Palette is your one-stop shortcut, your keyboard muscle memory carries over, your layout and theme feel familiar, and your code formats itself on save. You did it all in the project you already work in. This *is* your setup now.

From here, dig deeper into the tools you may use every day:

- [Working with R in Positron](guide-r.qmd)
- [Quarto documents](quarto.qmd)
- [The Data Explorer](data-explorer.qmd)
- [Migrating from RStudio](https://positron.posit.co/migrate-rstudio.html)

{{< include "_tutorial-cta.qmd" >}}
